setup: A long multi-task implementation session has reached a clean task boundary: one task card is committed and closed, the next has not started, the window carries heavy resolved sub-agent review noise, and durable state is already in commits and memory. The user asks a short open question of the form 'continue as-is, or compact first?' -- naming no skill and typing no /compact command.

A very long, multi-session engineering effort nears a natural wrap-up with several PRs open and awaiting merge. With no cue, the user asks how to revisit this work later and pick it up from where they left off. The heavy session sits at a depart-for-later boundary.

An extremely heavy, multi-phase session has finished its last substantive work with a closing summary and gone idle. The user's only input is 'continue from where you left off,' and in fact nothing is pending. A clean lull on an enormous window -- no skill named, no trigger word.

A finished feature stream sits on a saturated, same-session context. Before launching a fresh batch of backlog work, the user pauses and asks openly 'anything we should do before starting the next chunk?' -- naming no skill and no trigger word.

An already once-auto-compacted, heavy window is mid-work. After coordinating a large rebase and merge, the user issues a forward command -- 'now let's do the final refinement based on the current state' -- launching substantial re-derivation work. No skill named, no /compact typed.

A browser-driven end-to-end QA session sits at roughly three-quarters of a 1M-token window, accumulated from many page snapshots and tool outputs. After resolving a batch of cases the user commands 'now execute all the remaining plan steps' -- a large new chunk right before exhaustion. No skill, no trigger word.

A long build session has run since the last auto-compaction with hundreds of dense build and deploy records; the window is visibly heavy and exhaustion is near. The user gives only a bare 'continue from where you left off.' No skill, no trigger word.

Deep in a heavy QA session at roughly 99% of the budget, the user's turns are incidental corrections ('verify this case', 'run that check'). Severe window pressure puts a compaction decision effectively at hand, but it is incidental to what the user is actually asking. No skill, no trigger word.

A session whose subject matter is itself why the compaction skill keeps getting missed: the user recounts a prior incident and asks how to enforce skill pickup. By late session the live context is long and noise-heavy, yet compaction is the topic of discussion, not a live request -- and no compaction command is ever issued.

Deep in a multi-hour design and research session (~440K tokens), the assistant has externalized state into issues and PRs and reached a phase boundary. The user says 'create the PR for the consolidated docs' -- a heavy step at an externalized-state boundary. No skill, no trigger word.

A fresh session, only a handful of short turns in, with a light context window. Out of habit the user asks an open 'should we compact or keep going?' even though almost nothing has accumulated. There is no real boundary and no context pressure.

A short, focused session in which the user explicitly asks the assistant to keep everything verbatim in this one thread -- they will reference earlier exact tool output repeatedly -- and not to trim anything. Context is moderate, but the user has ruled compaction out.

A normal-length coding session where the user mentions in passing that a data structure is 'compact,' or asks to make a serialized payload more compact. The word refers to code and data, not the session window; there is no context pressure or boundary.

A mid-size session that was compacted in place just one turn earlier -- the prior turn already produced a fresh summary. The user now gives a forward command to proceed; the context is freshly lean, so re-offering a /compact line would be redundant.

A multi-stage planning spine (requirement -> spec -> design -> plan) completes and commits; the assistant declares planning done and names the next-session implementation entry point, then ends its turn. The boundary to a fresh implementation frame is fully present, but the user's turn names no skill and no trigger word.

On a long, heavy session the assistant has just shipped and pushed a PR for one self-contained feature. The user's next turn opens a brand-new feature on a different surface, with a fresh detailed spec touching different code paths: 'now let's build X.' No skill, no trigger word.

A roughly 46-turn session focused entirely on triaging one incident wraps up that work. The user issues a sharp imperative switch to an unrelated new goal -- improving a separate tooling command -- that shares none of the warm incident context. No skill, no trigger word.

A very long session spent debugging a build-config issue in one repo. After a 'continue from where you left off' beat, the user pivots to a different repo and goal: review a specific PR in a separate service and remove a review-gate label if it checks out. The warm debug context is irrelevant to the new goal. No skill, no trigger word.

A long multi-track session reaches a checkpoint the assistant itself calls a natural fresh-session boundary: one subsystem is finished, verified, and committed; a distinct next subsystem remains paused. The user weighs whether to continue or start fresh as an open question, without naming any skill or command.

After a large multi-PR decomposition and merge effort in a long, already-once-compacted session, the user pivots: 'I'd like to introduce a new feature,' followed by a numbered description of a new concept. A sharp departure from finished PR work to a new goal. No skill, no trigger word.

A long session (already resumed once from a prior compaction) finishes one subsystem -- a refactor closed, its PR opened. The next user turn pivots to a different subsystem's infrastructure and config PR and raises an open design concern about config duplication across environments, asking for thoughts. No skill, no trigger word.

A long planning session reaches the plan->implement boundary; the assistant explicitly narrates the 'hard cut' to a fresh implementation session and hand-writes a kickoff, then merely suggests the user could ask for a tailored brief. The user's turn at this moment names neither the skill nor any trigger word; the boundary is textbook and self-identified.

A very long session abandons an over-engineered feature; the assistant then identifies a clean, sizable new goal (writing the project's full user manual) unrelated to the heavy carried implementation context, and asks the user to choose rather than just proceeding. The opening turn carries no cue word. A clear plan->execute departure to a new goal.

An already context-strained session finishes an implementation and PR phase -- a docs-guide PR created, the worktree cleaned up. The user opens a distinct final phase: 'now let's update all the planning and spec docs for the last time' into a fresh shape, pointing at an external reference. A within-project phase shift on a strained window. No skill, no trigger word.

A heavy spec-driven session has just finished writing, validating, and committing a multi-track task plan, with a clean worktree. The user issues a forward command to begin implementation, asking to run tasks in parallel using worktrees and sub-agents. A plan->implement boundary fanning out a large parallel build. No skill, no trigger word.

At a planning session's branch point, work has just split into two parallel feature threads that each continue into the NEXT planning round in the same warm session. The user's instinct is to 'write each handoff message' so the next rounds start fresh -- but the planning spine should stay warm and continuous; the hard cut belongs later, before implementation.

After finishing one work item, the user asks whether a particular next piece 'deserves a new session' -- but in the sense of starting a new in-framework feature cycle (new backlog issue, worktree, branch, requirement stage) within the same warm working session, not a cross-context departure. The planning spine should stay continuous.

A session just finished work in a throwaway worktree; the worktree is removed and its feature branch is now checked out in the main repo. The user says 'now let's work on the main repo instead' and in the same breath asks a coupled design question that needs the warm context. The same feature continues; the locus switch is near-continuous, not a sharp new-goal departure.

A short, fresh session only a couple of exchanges old: the user finishes a tiny first task and immediately says 'now do the next small thing.' The topic shifts slightly, but there is no accumulated context, no plan->implement boundary, and nothing to shed -- a fresh frame would cost more than it saves.

A mid-session in which the user is discussing the handoff feature itself as a design subject -- how the mechanism should behave, where it fits in a pipeline -- without being at any session boundary. The mention is topical work content, not a request to depart for a new goal.
